text: Welcome to another episode of robotic hernia surgery.
Today, we're going to review a recent operation I performed on a patient, an ETEP unilateral transversus abdominis release.
This is a fifty-two year old man with an incarcerated recurrent ventral hernia.
He had previously presented with a concern for strangulation under emergency exploratory laparotomy with primary hernia repair.
I optimized him for some time. He lost some weight. He quit smoking, and he had his diabetes much better controlled. And then we planned an operation.

Cross-sectional imaging was obtained per usual with a hernia that measured about nine by twelve. He was a very large man. He was a retro rectus width on either side of nearly fourteen centimeters. So I thought that ETEP would be a great option for him.

The surgeon applies a left retromuscular access using an ETEP approach, inserting the applied medical first entry trocar to insufflate while the inner cannula is in place. The trocar punches through the muscle, allowing the surgeon to flatten their hands and enter the retromuscular space for pneumodissection. This technique quickly develops the space to facilitate port placement near the semilunar line.
The surgeon places the entry trocar centrally in the rectus muscle, ensuring consistent access for the procedure.
The surgeon incises the posterior sheath during the crossover phase of the ETEP surgery.

The surgeon enters the preperitoneal fat pad on the patient's left side, approximately one centimeter from the linea alba. The incision is made into the contralateral posterior rectus sheath, with no signs of previous upper abdominal surgery visible.

The surgeon carefully clears away the peritoneum to prevent injury to the underlying viscera, followed by performing a contralateral retro rectus dissection in the upper abdomen. The healthy tissue is visible, with the focus on addressing the hernia.
The surgeon continues to develop the retromuscular space, ensuring adequate separation of tissue layers to maintain clear visualization before addressing the hernia.
The surgeon approaches the cephalic component of the hernia, revealing incarcerated omentum with bowel nearby, and proceeds to enter the hernia sac.

In my previous videos, I have talked about entering the hernia sac almost as if it's a failure. I've recalibrated that recommendation. I think it's okay to enter the hernia sac and ETEP is not meant to be completely extraperitoneal. It's just the way that we initiate this operation.
The surgeon incises into the thin hernia sac, allowing the viscera to drop away. The incarcerated omentum and multifocal hernia sacs are visible in close proximity.
The surgeon extracts tissue from the hernia sac, gradually pulling it out over the course of the operation.
The surgeon identifies the defects in the hernia sac and begins to develop a retromuscular space. A large posterior hole is visible, resulting from the thinness of the hernia sac.
The surgeon recruits autologous tissue by dissecting the posterior sheath, progressing towards the space of Retzius, and developing the contralateral side.

The posterior sheath is not fully mobilized, and insufflation confirms deep paralysis. The surgeon ensures adequate retro rectus dissection and prepares to close the defect, deciding to extend the procedure with a unilateral transversus abdominis release (TAR) due to anticipated tension on the anterior structures.

The surgeon incises the posterior lamella of the internal abdominal oblique, medial to the perforators, exposing the transversus abdominis muscle. The transversus abdominis is then transected as part of step two of the ATAR procedure, preparing for dissection in the pre-transversal space.

The surgeon uses cautery and dissection techniques to mobilize the posterior sheath away from the underlying muscle, ensuring the transverse salus and peritoneum are preserved. The dissection progresses downward, aiming to expose landmarks such as the retroperitoneal fat pad, which is not yet visible in the video.

The surgeon maintains the dissection plane in the pre-transversalis space, with the transversalis fascia prominently visible and elevated with the muscle. The surgeon cuts into the transversalis fascia, which is located screen left, revealing the pre-peritoneal area.

The surgeon incises the same muscle, identifying the arcuate line, and proceeds into the preperitoneal space. The dissection continues to mobilize the sheath, resulting in a visibly lax posterior sheath.
You saw the anterior and for this case I ended up closing the posterior first. Again, I personally do a visceral sac closure. I do not try to

The surgeon demonstrates suturing techniques for closing the posterior sheath, using a baseball style stitch to carefully re-approximate the edges. The posterior sheath is thoroughly surveyed to ensure no holes or potential issues are missed.

The surgeon identifies and closes a hole in the peritoneum lower down. The anterior fascial defect is being closed, with much of the fascia and diastasis already placated. The surgeon approaches the hernia site.

The surgeon uses a permanent V-Lock suture on a GS-XXI needle, employing a double pulley system with the left hand moving up and the right hand moving down to facilitate efficient suturing. The initial run lightly brings the tissue together without final tightening, providing redundancy for subsequent runs to progressively re-approximate the fascia.

The surgeon sequentially tightens the sutures around the multifocal hernia area, avoiding placating the pseudosac to ensure healthy tissue reconstruction of the linea alba. The suturing is performed from the bottom up, progressively bringing the tissue together.
The surgeon locks the V-lock suture in place, securing the fascia during the crossover. The procedure progresses to the lower portion, preparing for the placement of a 30 by 30 polypropylene mesh.

The surgeon positions a polypropylene mesh in a diamond orientation, measuring up to forty-three centimeters in length, but uses only thirty-eight centimeters. The mesh is secured to the tissue, and due to the bloodless dissection, no drain is placed. The procedure concludes with an uncomplicated course, allowing for same-day discharge.

I think it highlights the potential of robotic surgery in patients who traditionally are considered high risk for abdominal wall reconstruction. Thank you very much for watching and I hope that was educational.
